observations.text: Age: Seed states 34; interview confirms age 34 with DOB August 14, 1991 (Q85), and Maria references being thirty-four in Q03 ('I'm thirty-four now so that GED thing is getting real close'). Fully consistent.
Gender: Seed states Female; interview consistently presents Maria as a woman, mother, and wife throughout all responses (Q01, Q05, Q84, Q88). Fully consistent.
Marital status: Seed states married_present; interview confirms married twelve years to Carlos, living together with two children (Q05, Q84). Consistent.

Education: Seed states 9th_12th_no_diploma; interview specifies Maria dropped out during 11th grade ('my junior year') and has no diploma (Q01, Q90). Currently self-studying for GED (Q91). Consistent and enriched.

Occupation: Seed states no_occupation; interview confirms no formal employment -- Maria describes herself as at-home with children, performing informal catering and volunteer event planning (Q16, Q65, Q76). Consistent.

Location: Seed states Lilburn, GA 30047; interview confirms lifelong residence in Lilburn, Gwinnett County, with detailed neighborhood descriptions (Q01, Q08, Q87, Q97). Born at hospital in Lawrenceville but raised in Lilburn. Fully consistent.

Household composition: Interview describes four-person household (Maria, Carlos age 36, Diego age 10, Sofia age 7) plus dog Canela (Q05, Q11, Q51). Extracted profile captures location and some household details in home_and_lifestyle but does not include a dedicated household section with all members' ages. Partial capture.

Rent amount and trajectory: Interview specifies $1,450/month rent, up from $1,100 five years ago (Q10, Q56, Q58). Profile accurately captures both current rent and the increase trajectory in home_and_lifestyle. Consistent.

Parents' education and father's details: Interview provides father's education (~6th-7th grade in Mexico, Q100), occupation (landscaping and construction, Q105-Q107), parents' separation (Q05), and father's current location ('other side of town,' Q05). Brother Luis's details (age 28, community college, car dealership, Q05). Profile's demographer summary mentions brother and mother but omits father's education level and current situation entirely.

PeachCare for children: Interview mentions kids on PeachCare, Georgia's children's health program (Q40, Q80). Also notes the family is marginally over SNAP income limits (Q80). Neither detail appears in the extracted profile.

Birth location: Interview states Maria was born at the hospital in Lawrenceville but grew up in Lilburn (Q87). Profile lists location as Lilburn, GA 30047 but does not note the Lawrenceville birth detail. Minor gap.

Age and life timeline: Profile states age 34, married 12 years (married at 22), met Carlos at 19. Diego is 10 (born when Maria was ~23, one year after marriage). Sofia is 7 (born when Maria was ~27). These timelines are internally consistent across the demographer summary and life_events_and_transitions.

Income figures: Profile states $400-$800/month catering income in work_and_career details. Demographer summary states the same range. Household income described as ~$3,200 from Carlos plus Maria's contribution. These are internally consistent across sections.

Education status: Profile states '11th grade, no diploma; self-studying for GED' in education field. Work_and_career preferences mention GED study and Gwinnett Tech. Life_events says 'plans to earn GED by age 35.' All internally consistent.

Father's background and current status: Interview provides father's education (~6th-7th grade in Mexico, Q100), occupation (landscaping and construction, Q105-Q107), and current relationship status with Maria (separated from mother when Maria was in her twenties, lives across town, 'we talk but it's not like it was,' Q05). None of this appears in the extracted profile.

Brother Luis's details: Interview identifies Luis as 28, finished school, attended community college, works at car dealership (Q05). He also serves as a political information source for Maria (Q23 follow-up). Not captured in any profile section.
Government benefits: PeachCare enrollment for children and SNAP ineligibility (marginally over income threshold, Q80) are significant economic details absent from the profile.

Specific debt and savings figures: Interview details $1,800 credit card debt, ~$600 medical debt, $200 owed to mother, $400 savings, $100-$150 emergency cash (Q61, Q63, Q82). Profile does not capture these specific figures in any section.

Mother's specific health situation: Interview describes mother's debilitating knee problems requiring potential replacement surgery she cannot afford, her age (62), and Maria's caregiving role (groceries, appointments, housework, 2-3 visits/week) (Q05, Q35). Profile's demographer summary mentions mother broadly but without these specifics.

Budgeting expertise: Seed mentions 'methodical budgeting expertise' and 'household budgeting and finance management.' Interview confirms through detailed descriptions of meal planning, coupon use, bulk purchasing, and Sunday night budget-planning sessions with Carlos (Q56, Q61, Q83 follow-up). Consistent and richly elaborated.
observations.source_a_ref: seed.skills_and_expertise: household budgeting and finance management

observations.text: Event planning and financial management: Seed describes event budgeting at an arts center. Interview confirms Maria managed a $2,000 budget for Dia de los Muertos, secured five business donations, coordinated twenty volunteers, and tracks all expenses in a notebook (Q02, Q02 follow-up, Q16, Q65). Consistent.

Catering business: Seed's career_goals mentions launching a home-based catering business. Interview confirms Maria is already doing this informally, charging $150-$600 per event, earning $400-$800/month (Q03 follow-up, Q16, Q72). The seed frames it as aspiration; the interview reveals it is partially realized.

Travel and leisure spending: Seed's travel_persona describes weekend road trips to Appalachian foothills, annual cultural escapes to Savannah's historic district, and occasional European getaways to Barcelona and Lisbon. Interview reveals zero discretionary travel beyond local outings; the only notable trip was Six Flags using tax refund money (Q75). Budget constraints render seed travel aspirations implausible for current economic situation.

Certification pursuit: Seed's professional_persona states Maria is 'actively pursuing certification in community development.' Interview reveals she has not enrolled in any program (Q91, Q92) and is self-studying for GED only with library workbooks and YouTube (Q18, Q91). The seed overstates the formality and stage of her educational pursuit.

Cost-effective decision-making: Seed mentions Maria finding 'cost-effective solutions.' Interview provides multiple concrete examples: shopping at Mexican markets for cheaper produce, growing garden herbs, buying rice/beans/flour in bulk, stretching one chicken into three days of soup, clipping coupons (Q15 follow-up, Q17 follow-up, Q56 follow-up). Consistent and deeply elaborated.
observations.source_b_ref: Q15 follow-up, Q17 follow-up, Q56 follow-up

observations.text: Loss aversion around savings: Interview describes Maria's distress at depleting the $400 savings buffer, the rule with Carlos to maintain at least $200, and the feeling of 'going backwards' when withdrawing (Q61, Q82). Profile's behavioral_economist summary accurately captures this as 'strong loss aversion around her small savings buffer' and 'acute psychological distress at the prospect of depleting it.' Faithfully represented.

Income shock response: Interview details Maria's multi-pronged response to Carlos's reduced hours: selling tamales through Facebook ($10/dozen, ~30 dozen sold), borrowing $200 from mother, cutting all discretionary spending including yoga, cancelling non-essentials (Q60, Q81). Profile's behavioral_economist summary mentions all these strategies. Accurately captured.

Health care trade-offs: Interview reveals Maria skipped her own checkups for two years to fund children's appointments, refused ER visit during 3-day headache due to cost, and owes $600 from Sofia's ear infection (Q36, Q40, Q63). Profile accurately characterizes this as 'systematic self-sacrifice pattern in household resource allocation.' Faithfully captured.

Tax refund behavior: Interview describes the $2,800 refund as a critical annual mechanism for catching up -- paying credit card, saving, kids' clothes, porch repair, Six Flags trip (Q75). This important economic pattern is absent from the profile.

Credit card debt and minimum-payment trap: Maria acknowledges paying minimums on ~$1,800 in credit card debt and recognizes the interest problem (Q63). This specific debt instrument and its compounding cost are not captured in the profile.

Sunday night financial planning ritual: Interview reveals Carlos and Maria sit down Sunday nights to review upcoming bills and expenses (Q83 follow-up). This structured household financial management behavior is not captured in the profile.

Catering income range: Profile's work_and_career section says $400-$800/month from catering. The specific month reported in Q72 yielded $650 from catering plus $100 arts center stipend, consistent with the range. Behavioral_economist summary describes the same entrepreneurial pattern. Internally consistent.

GED as human capital investment: Behavioral_economist summary calls GED pursuit 'a calculated human capital investment despite high opportunity cost.' Work_and_career preferences describe self-study. Life_events mentions timeline goal (by age 35). All three sections consistently characterize the same pursuit from complementary angles.

Budget stretching behaviors: Behavioral_economist summary mentions 'meticulously manages food costs through bulk buying, garden cultivation, and strategic shopping at ethnic markets.' Food_and_dining preferences list the same strategies. No contradiction between the economic analysis and the preference detail.

Monthly expense breakdown: Interview provides detailed monthly costs -- $1,450 rent, $320 car payment, ~$250 utilities, $500-$600 groceries, $50 medical payment, plus gas and vehicle insurance (Q56, Q57). Profile does not capture this granular expense data anywhere.

Seasonal expense fluctuation: Interview describes summer electric bills of $180-$200, Christmas costing extra $300-$400, back-to-school expenses in August for clothes, supplies, and activity fees (Q59). Profile does not capture seasonal budget variation.

Informal lending networks: Interview reveals bidirectional lending -- borrowed $200 from mother (Q63, Q81), $200 from Lupita for car brakes (Q64). Profile's behavioral_economist summary mentions 'family lending networks' generally but does not capture specific amounts or reciprocity patterns.

Carlos's income variability: Interview details Carlos earning ~$3,200/month after taxes but hours subject to weather delays and construction cycles, with income dropping significantly during cut-back periods (Q38, Q74, Q81). Profile mentions husband's wages but not the variability or its economic impact.

Filing taxes jointly: Interview reveals they file jointly using Carlos's brother-in-law as informal tax preparer, receive ~$2,800 refund primarily from child tax credits, which functions as critical annual catch-up mechanism (Q75). Not captured.

Community organizing: Seed's professional_persona describes Maria designing 'inclusive festivals, workshops, and cultural programs for a local arts center.' Interview confirms extensive event planning: Dia de los Muertos festival with $2,000 budget and 20 volunteers (Q02), spring art walk (Q16, Q71), neighborhood potlucks drawing diverse cultural groups (Q08), church festivals and Las Posadas (Q47). Consistent and deeply elaborated.

Civic engagement: Seed mentions no political or civic engagement. Interview reveals Maria is a registered voter since age 26-27 (Q23), attends county commission meetings (Q25), discusses race and policing issues in her community (Q26-Q30), and is engaged with local zoning debates (Q25). Interview significantly enriches a civic dimension absent from seed.

Social media community moderation: Seed lists 'social media community moderation' as a skill. Interview confirms Maria moderates three Facebook groups (Lilburn neighborhood, Mexican families in Gwinnett, arts center events) and actively connects community members with resources such as Spanish-speaking doctors and mechanics (Q48, Q49). Consistent and enriched.

Cross-cultural bridge-building: Seed's cultural_background mentions 'fostering cross-cultural exchange in her everyday life.' Interview confirms multicultural potlucks (Korean BBQ next to tamales next to samosas, Q08 follow-up), diverse book club membership, and inclusive event planning philosophy ('I want everybody to feel welcome, not just Mexican people,' Q29). Consistent.

Church as institutional hub: Seed mentions 'Catholic upbringing influences her participation in church activities, which serve as a hub for both spiritual and social connections.' Interview confirms extensive church involvement at St. Patrick's in Lilburn: volunteering, event planning, youth programs, Las Posadas, Dia de los Muertos altars, and Father Morales as trusted community figure and information source for vaccination (Q42, Q47). Fully consistent.

Voting behavior: Interview specifies Maria registered at 26-27, votes in presidential elections, inconsistently in smaller races due to information costs ('I try to look stuff up before I go but I don't always have time,' Q23). Profile's political_scientist summary accurately describes this as 'inconsistently in lower-ballot races, reflecting an information-cost barrier rather than apathy.' Faithfully represented.

County commission attendance: Interview describes Maria attending 4-5 county meetings after being introduced by Janet from book club (Q25, Q25 follow-up). Profile's community_and_civic preferences and political_scientist summary both capture this. Consistent.

Political identity complexity: Interview reveals Maria is issue-driven rather than party-identified, leans toward the party supporting working families and health care, but holds traditional family values on social issues and disagrees with her preferred side on some social matters (Q24, Q95, Q95 follow-up, Q96). Profile accurately describes this as 'economically progressive, culturally traditional, institutionally pragmatic.' Well captured.

Immigration-related awareness and policing anxiety: Interview reveals a persistent background anxiety about documentation and policing for the Mexican community, including neighbors who avoid calling police due to status fears (Q20, Q26 follow-up). Maria ensures family documents are in order and instructs Carlos to cooperate with police (Q20). Profile mentions 'fair treatment of immigrant-origin families' as a political issue but does not capture the day-to-day dimension of this awareness.

Information sources for political decisions: Interview describes Maria consulting trusted network members (Lupita, brother Luis, church ladies) and phone research for voting decisions, explicitly avoiding party-line thinking (Q23 follow-up). Profile does not capture these specific information-seeking pathways for political decision-making.

Civic engagement characterization: Profile's community_and_civic preferences list 'recently started attending Gwinnett County commission meetings' and 'registered voter; votes in presidential and some local elections.' The political_scientist summary describes an 'engagement trajectory moving from disengagement to increasing participation.' These are consistent -- the preferences describe behavior, the summary explains the trend.

Facebook community management as civic infrastructure: Profile's technology preferences describe managing three Facebook groups. Political_scientist summary characterizes this as 'informal civic infrastructure even though she does not frame it politically.' These assessments are compatible and complementary.

Cultural traditionalism and social progressivism: Political_scientist summary says 'economically progressive, culturally traditional.' Psychologist summary references faith and family values as core identity components. Both summaries acknowledge the same values-tension without contradicting each other.

Carlos's political stance: Interview describes Carlos as politically disengaged ('just let me work and take care of my family') but occasionally fired up about construction worker or immigration issues (Q24 follow-up). This household political dynamic is absent from the profile.

Generational divide on racial justice in Mexican community: Interview describes older generation's reluctance to engage ('that's their business') versus younger generation's vocal support, with Maria positioning herself in between (Q27 follow-up, Q30). This intracommunity dynamic is absent from the profile.

Neighbor's college-age daughter as political influence: Interview identifies this young woman as someone who taught Maria about solidarity and intersectionality, and whom Maria encouraged to run for office someday (Q30 follow-up). This cross-generational political learning relationship is absent from the profile.

School funding as political catalyst: Interview reveals that seeing Diego's teacher buying supplies out of her own pocket was the catalyst for Maria's political awakening and voter registration (Q23 follow-up). This specific motivating experience is not captured in the political_scientist summary.

Caretaking as core identity: Seed's persona describes Maria channeling 'curiosity and methodical energy into weaving art, food, and community together.' Interview reveals a deeply embedded caretaker pattern originating in childhood -- caring for brother after school while mother worked two jobs (Q01), extending through adulthood as caregiver to mother (Q05, Q35), children, church community, and neighborhood (Q17, Q44, Q109). Maria's self-concept is structured around being needed. Consistent with seed's community-focused framing but significantly deeper.
Kitchen gadget hoarding: Seed mentions Maria 'occasionally hoards seldom-used kitchen gadgets.' This specific behavioral detail does not appear in the interview. Neither confirmed nor contradicted.

Forgetting to water garden during busy weeks: Seed mentions this habit. Interview confirms it directly -- 'Sometimes I forget to water everything when I'm really busy with event planning and stuff comes back kind of sad looking' (Q17 follow-up). Consistent.

Artistic engagement breadth: Seed's arts_persona mentions watercolor, pottery, folk dance, specific artist inspirations (O'Keeffe, Monet, Pollock), and a multicultural book club with Morrison, Murakami, and Adichie. Interview confirms folk dance (Q01, Q15), book club with Morrison and Adichie (Q07), watercolor and pottery workshops at the School of the Arts (from seed, not explicitly in interview). The specific artist names and Murakami did not surface in the interview.

Cooking as emotional regulation: Seed mentions Maria 'channels curiosity and methodical energy' through food. Interview vividly confirms cooking as a primary coping mechanism -- making 60 tamales at midnight during financial stress, the kitchen described as 'like therapy for me' (Q38, Q38 follow-up). Behavioral pattern deeply consistent.

Co-ed recreational soccer: Seed's sports_persona states Maria 'participates in a co-ed recreational soccer league each spring.' Interview contains no mention of Maria playing soccer -- only her son Diego plays in a rec league (Q05, Q19). This seed element appears unsubstantiated by the interview.

Emotional breakdown episode: Interview provides a detailed account of the fall 2025 crisis -- sitting on bathroom floor crying, feeling overwhelmed by converging financial stress, Diego's bullying, and mother's health episode (Q44). Profile's psychologist summary accurately captures this as revealing 'her emotional regulation system can be overwhelmed when multiple role demands converge simultaneously.' Faithfully represented.

Nighttime rumination pattern: Interview describes lying awake running through financial worries, children's futures, and mother's health (Q43, Q43 follow-up). Profile captures this in sensory_and_physical_context ('poor sleep quality: lies awake ruminating') and psychologist summary ('Neuroticism manifests as nighttime rumination'). Accurately represented.

Full coping mechanism inventory: Interview identifies five coping strategies: cooking as emotional regulation (Q38), yoga breathing for chest tightness (Q45 follow-up), garden time as solitary restoration (Q43 follow-up), weekly check-in texts with Lupita (Q44 follow-up), and Mass attendance as centering ritual (Q44 follow-up, Q47). Profile's psychologist summary names only cooking, yoga breathing, and Lupita -- omitting garden time and Mass as explicit coping mechanisms.
observations.source_a_ref: Q38, Q43 follow-up, Q44 follow-up, Q45 follow-up, Q47

observations.text: Self-concept evolution from helper to leader: Interview traces Maria's shift from 'just a helper' to recognizing herself as capable of leading after the Dia de los Muertos festival: 'I was like, wait, I actually ran this whole thing... People came to me with questions and I had answers' (Q02 follow-up). Profile's psychologist summary captures the tension between caretaker identity and emerging self-investment. Faithfully represented.

Children's behavioral stress responses: Interview describes Diego becoming withdrawn and drawing for hours, Sofia becoming clingy when household tension rises (Q46). This family stress-response pattern is entirely absent from the profile.

Recurrent guilt pattern: Interview reveals Maria feels guilt when she cannot provide shoes for Diego (Q64 follow-up: 'Like I'm failing'), guilt when others cover for her during illness (Q33 follow-up: 'I felt so guilty that everybody had to pick up my slack'), and guilt about 'dropping the ball' as a mother (Q43). This consistent guilt pattern is not explicitly named in the psychologist summary.

Conscientiousness characterization: Psychologist summary describes 'high Conscientiousness through meticulous event budgeting, meal planning, and household management.' Behavioral_economist summary describes the same meticulous budgeting and planning. Cross-expert consistency confirmed.

Openness to Experience: Psychologist summary states this is 'notable in her artistic pursuits (dance, watercolor, pottery, multicultural reading) and willingness to experiment with fusion cooking, though constrained by economic realities.' Hobbies_and_recreation and arts_and_culture preference sections list all these activities. Consistent.

Relational style: Psychologist summary says 'clear attachment to a small number of deep relationships (Lupita, mother, Ana) rather than broad shallow networks.' Social_and_relationships preferences list the same core relationships while also noting broader community ties (book club, church, Facebook groups). The psychologist correctly distinguishes depth from breadth. Internally consistent.

Guilt as recurrent emotional pattern: Interview reveals guilt when unable to provide materially (Q64 follow-up), guilt when others must compensate during her illness (Q33 follow-up), and guilt about perceived parenting failures (Q43). This consistent pattern warrants explicit naming in the psychologist domain.

Cultural barriers shifting toward practical barriers in help-seeking: Interview describes Maria's awareness of her culture's stigma against therapy ('you're supposed to be strong, pray about it,' Q45) but notes her own barrier has shifted from shame to practical obstacles (cost, time, Spanish-speaking availability, Q45 follow-up). This nuanced shift is not captured in the profile.

Motherhood as central motivational axis: Interview frames nearly every major decision through children's futures -- staying in neighborhood for schools, pursuing GED as modeling for children, wanting college for Diego and Sofia (Q01, Q43 follow-up, Q62, Q108, Q109). Profile touches this in multiple places but the psychologist summary does not foreground motherhood as the primary motivational driver.
observations.source_a_ref: Q01, Q43 follow-up, Q62, Q108, Q109

observations.text: Diego's ethnic bullying and Maria's protective advocacy: Interview details kids making 'jokes about tacos' at Diego, him coming home crying, Maria talking to his teacher, explaining to Diego to be proud of being Mexican (Q28, Q28 follow-up, Q44). This parenting challenge and cultural-identity transmission moment are absent from the profile.

Carlos's emotional pattern under stress: Interview describes Carlos as 'strong silent type' who gets 'even quieter than usual' and sleeps poorly when stressed, worrying about providing for the family (Q46). This partner dynamic shapes the household emotional environment but is absent from the profile.

expert_summaries.demographer: Maria Buendia is a 34-year-old US-born Mexican-American woman who has lived her entire life in Lilburn, Georgia (Gwinnett County), a diverse suburban community east of Atlanta (Q01, Q87, Q97). She was born at a hospital in Lawrenceville but raised in Lilburn (Q87). She is married to Carlos, age 36, a construction worker earning approximately $3,200/month after taxes, and they have two school-age children: Diego (10, 4th grade) and Sofia (7, 2nd grade), in a four-person household renting a three-bedroom home for $1,450/month (Q05, Q11, Q56, Q58). Her education stopped at 11th grade with no diploma when she dropped out to help care for her younger brother while her mother worked two jobs (Q01, Q90). She has no formal occupation and relies on informal catering income of $400-$800/month plus small arts center stipends, supplementing Carlos's wages (Q16, Q72, Q74). The household income of $3,000-$4,000/month places them near the lower-middle-income threshold for a family of four in the Atlanta metro area, with children enrolled in PeachCare but the family marginally over SNAP income limits (Q40, Q80). Her parents immigrated from Mexico before her birth; her father (~6th-7th grade education, former landscaper/construction worker) separated from her mother when Maria was in her twenties and lives across town (Q05, Q98, Q100, Q105-Q107). Her mother Rosa, age 62, lives 15 minutes away with debilitating knee problems, requiring Maria's caregiving support 2-3 times weekly (Q05, Q35). Her brother Luis, 28, completed community college and works at a car dealership (Q05).
expert_summaries.behavioral_economist: Maria's economic behavior reflects constrained optimization under persistent scarcity rather than preference-driven choices (Q56, Q61, Q83). She demonstrates strong loss aversion around her small savings buffer of approximately $400, with a joint household rule to maintain at least $200 as a floor, and describes acute distress when forced to deplete it (Q61, Q61 follow-up, Q82). Her household carries $1,800 in credit card debt (minimum payments only), $600 in medical debt from Sofia's ear infection, and $200 owed to her mother, against $400 in savings and $100-$150 in emergency cash (Q63, Q61). Her informal catering business demonstrates entrepreneurial resourcefulness: she scales labor supply based on need, including selling tamales at $10/dozen through Facebook during income shocks (Q81), uses social networks for client acquisition, and meticulously manages food costs through bulk purchasing, garden cultivation, and strategic shopping at ethnic markets on Buford Highway (Q56 follow-up). Her decision to forgo personal health care for over two years -- skipping checkups, refusing an ER visit during a three-day headache, delaying back pain treatment -- while maintaining children's PeachCare coverage reveals systematic self-sacrifice in household resource allocation (Q36, Q40). She and Carlos conduct Sunday-night financial planning sessions reviewing upcoming bills (Q83 follow-up), and the annual tax refund of approximately $2,800 functions as a critical catch-up mechanism for deferred needs (Q75).
expert_summaries.political_scientist: Maria exhibits a civic engagement trajectory moving from youthful disengagement to increasing participation, catalyzed by parental stakes in public institutions -- specifically, witnessing Diego's teacher buying classroom supplies out of pocket (Q23, Q23 follow-up). She registered to vote at 26-27 and participates in presidential elections but inconsistently in lower-ballot races, reflecting information-cost barriers rather than apathy; she researches candidates through trusted personal networks including Lupita, brother Luis, and church contacts (Q23, Q23 follow-up). Her political orientation is issue-driven rather than party-identified, clustering around affordable health care, adequately funded schools, cost of living, and fair treatment of immigrant-origin families, while holding culturally traditional positions on family and faith that create cross-pressures (Q24, Q95, Q96). She recently expanded her civic repertoire to include attending 4-5 Gwinnett County commission meetings, prompted by book club member Janet (Q25, Q25 follow-up). Her community organizing work -- moderating three Facebook groups, planning multicultural events, organizing neighborhood potlucks that bring together Korean, Mexican, Indian, Black, and white families (Q08, Q48) -- functions as informal civic infrastructure. Her response to racial justice movements is empathetic and solidarity-oriented, acknowledging shared experiences of discrimination between Mexican and Black communities, but behaviorally cautious, promoting inclusion through events and private conversations rather than protest (Q26, Q27, Q29).
expert_summaries.psychologist: Maria displays high conscientiousness through meticulous event budgeting, meal planning, and household management, paired with high agreeableness evident in her lifelong caretaking across family and community spheres -- a pattern originating in childhood when she became 'a second mom in the house' after dropping out of school to care for her brother (Q01, Q01 follow-up). Her self-concept centers on a caretaker identity that extends to her mother, children, church community, and neighborhood, with a persistent guilt pattern emerging when she cannot meet these demands -- expressing feelings of 'failing' when unable to buy Diego shoes (Q64 follow-up) and guilt when others compensate during her illness (Q33 follow-up). A critical tension exists between this caretaker orientation and her emerging recognition of self-investment needs, catalyzed by the Dia de los Muertos festival that shifted her self-perception from 'just a helper' to a capable leader (Q02 follow-up). Her emotional regulation system handles routine stress through five functional coping mechanisms: cooking as emotional processing (Q38), yoga breathing for acute stress (Q45 follow-up), garden time as solitary restoration (Q43 follow-up), weekly honest check-in texts with Lupita (Q44 follow-up), and Mass attendance as spiritual centering (Q44 follow-up, Q47). However, when multiple role demands converge simultaneously -- as during the fall 2025 crisis combining financial stress, Diego's ethnic bullying at school, and her mother's health episode -- her system can become overwhelmed, as evidenced by the bathroom-floor crying episode where she was unable to speak when Carlos found her (Q44). Her attitude toward professional help has shifted from cultural stigma to practical barriers (cost, time, Spanish-speaking availability), reflecting generational change in her community (Q45, Q45 follow-up). Motherhood is her primary motivational axis: nearly every major decision is framed through her children's futures, from staying in the neighborhood for school continuity to pursuing her GED as modeling behavior for Diego and Sofia (Q01, Q108, Q109).

inconsistencies_found.description: Seed's travel_persona describes active travel habits (Appalachian road trips, annual Savannah escapes, European getaways to Barcelona and Lisbon) that are financially implausible given interview-revealed household economics of $3,000-$4,000/month income with $400 in savings. Only local outings and one Six Flags trip (using tax refund) were reported in the interview.
inconsistencies_found.source_refs: seed.travel_persona vs. Q10, Q56, Q57, Q64, Q75

inconsistencies_found.description: Seed's professional_persona states Maria is 'actively pursuing certification in community development,' but interview reveals she has not enrolled in any program (Q91, Q92) and is only self-studying for GED with library workbooks and YouTube tutorials (Q18). The certification is a future aspiration contingent on GED completion, not an active pursuit.

Seed's sports_persona states Maria 'participates in a co-ed recreational soccer league each spring.' Interview contains no mention of Maria playing soccer herself -- only her son Diego plays in a rec league (Q05, Q19). This seed element appears unsubstantiated and may be aspirational or a past activity.

resolutions.resolution: Travel preferences are reframed as aspirational seed data that does not reflect current economic reality. The corrected profile marks these explicitly as seed-derived aspirations rather than active travel patterns. Interview-supported local activities (parks, Buford Highway, Six Flags) are foregrounded.

The corrected profile establishes the GED as the current active educational pursuit, with community development certification positioned as a subsequent goal contingent on GED completion. The word 'actively pursuing' is replaced with accurate staging.

Personal soccer league participation is moved to the unresolved_or_ambiguous section with a note that it was not confirmed in the interview. The sports_and_fitness preference section is corrected to reflect only interview-supported activities.

Brother Luis's demographic details (age 28, community college, car dealership) and role as political information source added to social_and_relationships and political_scientist contexts.
PeachCare enrollment for children and SNAP ineligibility (marginally over income threshold) added to home_and_lifestyle details.
Specific debt figures ($1,800 credit card, $600 medical, $200 to mother) and savings ($400 + $100-$150 cash) added to home_and_lifestyle financial details.
Monthly expense breakdown ($1,450 rent, $320 car, ~$250 utilities, $500-$600 groceries, $50 medical) added to home_and_lifestyle.
Seasonal expense variation (summer electric $180-$200, Christmas +$300-$400, back-to-school in August) noted in home_and_lifestyle.
Tax refund behavior ($2,800, used as annual catch-up mechanism) added to home_and_lifestyle.
Children's behavioral stress responses (Diego withdrawing/drawing, Sofia becoming clingy) added to others_misc sensory_and_physical_context.
Maria's guilt pattern added to psychologist summary.
Cultural barriers shifting to practical barriers for professional help-seeking added to psychologist summary.
Diego's ethnic bullying experience and Maria's protective advocacy response added to others_misc life_events_and_transitions.
Carlos's emotional pattern under stress (withdrawing, sleeping poorly) added to others_misc sensory_and_physical_context.
Garden time and Mass attendance explicitly listed as coping mechanisms in psychologist summary.
Immigration-related awareness and policing anxiety added to political_scientist summary.
School funding as political catalyst added to political_scientist summary.
Carlos's political stance (disengaged but occasionally fired up on construction/immigration) noted in community_and_civic.
Sunday-night financial planning sessions with Carlos noted in home_and_lifestyle.
Informal lending network specifics (mother, Lupita, amounts) captured in home_and_lifestyle.
verification.final_status: PASS_WITH_REVISIONS. The interview is richly consistent with seed data across all four expert domains. Three minor inconsistencies were identified between seed aspirational content and interview-revealed economic reality (travel habits, certification status, soccer league). The extracted_profile faithfully represents the interview across most domains but has completeness gaps in financial granularity (debts, savings, expense breakdown, seasonal variation, tax refund), family demographics (father's background, brother Luis's details), government benefits (PeachCare, SNAP ineligibility), and several behavioral patterns (guilt, children's stress responses, help-seeking attitudes, immigration awareness). All gaps are addressable through profile amendments captured in the corrected_extracted_profile below without requiring re-interview.

health_and_wellness.summary: Relies on weekly yoga, traditional Mexican herbal remedies, and an active lifestyle rather than formal medical care, largely due to cost barriers. Has not had a medical checkup in over two years.

Takes ibuprofen 2-3 times per week for chronic lower back pain (ongoing ~18 months)

Uses traditional Mexican herbal remedies passed down from mother and grandmother: chamomile (manzanilla) tea for sleep and stomach, mint tea for headaches, ginger tea for digestion, lime-honey-cinnamon tea for colds
Mother also practices sobadas (traditional abdominal massage) and egg limpia (passing raw egg to cleanse bad energy)
Grows herbs (cilantro, mint) in home garden partly for medicinal use
Has not had a checkup in over two years due to cost and time constraints
Suspects low iron based on mother's similar experience with afternoon fatigue; tries to eat more spinach and meat but has not gotten blood work done
Uses 4-count breathing technique from yoga to manage chest tightness during acute stress episodes (10-15 minute duration)
Takes birth control pills (only prescription medication)
Children on PeachCare (Georgia children's health program); Maria herself has minimal coverage
Uses community health center in Lawrenceville with sliding-scale fees when necessary, but long wait times (2-3 hours) make it impractical with school pickup schedule
Pro-vaccination: children fully vaccinated on schedule; Maria got COVID vaccine as soon as available

social_and_relationships.summary: Deeply embedded in family and community networks. Maintains close friendships, church ties, extended family connections, and organized social groups. Relational style favors a small number of deep attachments over broad shallow networks.
details: Husband Carlos, 36: construction worker, together 15 years, married 12. Described as dependable, quiet, 'my rock.' Watches sports together, co-parents, Sunday-night financial planning sessions
Son Diego, 10 (4th grade): smart, good grades especially in math and art, plays rec soccer, draws for hours, protective of sister. Experienced ethnic bullying at school
Daughter Sofia, 7 (2nd grade): outgoing, social leader in class, does hip-hop dance, talks a lot, catching up on reading. Preparing for First Communion
Dog Canela: chihuahua mix, beloved by the kids

Best friend Lupita (since childhood, lives in Norcross): talks almost daily by phone/text, shops together at Mexican market on Buford Highway, weekly honest check-in texts, mutual support including lending $200 for car brakes

Father: separated from mother when Maria was in her twenties, lives across town. Relationship is distant ('we talk but it's not like it was'). ~6th-7th grade education in Mexico, former landscaper and construction worker
Brother Luis, 28: finished school, attended community college, works at car dealership. Maria is proud of him. Serves as political information source
Sister-in-law Patricia: frequent phone calls, close relationship

summaries.demographer: Maria Buendia is a 34-year-old US-born Mexican-American woman who has lived her entire life in Lilburn, Georgia (Gwinnett County), a diverse suburban community east of Atlanta (Q01, Q87, Q97). She was born at a hospital in Lawrenceville but raised in Lilburn (Q87). She is married to Carlos, age 36, a construction worker earning approximately $3,200/month after taxes, and they have two school-age children: Diego (10, 4th grade) and Sofia (7, 2nd grade), in a four-person household renting a three-bedroom home for $1,450/month (Q05, Q11, Q56, Q58). Her education stopped at 11th grade with no diploma when she dropped out to help care for her younger brother while her mother worked two jobs (Q01, Q90). She has no formal occupation and relies on informal catering income of $400-$800/month plus small arts center stipends, supplementing Carlos's wages (Q16, Q72, Q74). The household income of $3,000-$4,000/month places them near the lower-middle-income threshold for a family of four in the Atlanta metro area, with children enrolled in PeachCare but the family marginally over SNAP income limits (Q40, Q80). Her parents immigrated from Mexico before her birth; her father (~6th-7th grade education in Mexico, former landscaper/construction worker) separated from her mother when Maria was in her twenties and lives across town with a distant relationship (Q05, Q98, Q100, Q105-Q107). Her mother Rosa, age 62, lives 15 minutes away with debilitating knee problems requiring potential replacement surgery she cannot afford, and Maria serves as her primary caregiver visiting 2-3 times weekly (Q05, Q35). Her brother Luis, 28, completed community college and works at a car dealership (Q05).
summaries.behavioral_economist: Maria's economic behavior reflects constrained optimization under persistent scarcity rather than preference-driven choices (Q56, Q61, Q83). She demonstrates strong loss aversion around her small savings buffer of approximately $400, with a joint household rule to maintain at least $200 as a floor, and describes acute distress when forced to deplete it (Q61, Q61 follow-up, Q82). Her household carries $1,800 in credit card debt (minimum payments only, aware of compounding interest), $600 in medical debt from Sofia's ear infection, and $200 owed to her mother, against $400 in savings and $100-$150 in emergency cash (Q63, Q61). Her informal catering business demonstrates entrepreneurial resourcefulness: she scales labor supply based on need -- selling tamales at $10/dozen through Facebook during income shocks, yielding approximately 30 dozen in one crisis month (Q81) -- uses social networks for client acquisition, and meticulously manages food costs through bulk purchasing, garden cultivation, couponing, and strategic shopping at ethnic markets on Buford Highway (Q56 follow-up). Her decision to forgo personal health care for over two years while maintaining children's PeachCare coverage reveals systematic self-sacrifice in household resource allocation; she refused an ER visit during a three-day headache and delays back pain and blood work due to cost (Q36, Q40). She and Carlos conduct Sunday-night financial planning sessions reviewing upcoming bills and expenses (Q83 follow-up). The annual tax refund of approximately $2,800, primarily from child tax credits, functions as the household's critical catch-up mechanism for deferred needs including debt reduction, savings replenishment, and deferred purchases (Q75). She responds to income shocks (Carlos's weather-related hour cuts) with immediate multi-pronged behavioral adjustments: increasing informal labor supply, cutting all discretionary spending (including yoga class), and activating bidirectional lending networks with her mother ($200) and Lupita ($200 for car brakes) (Q60, Q64, Q81).
summaries.political_scientist: Maria exhibits a civic engagement trajectory moving from youthful disengagement to increasing participation, catalyzed by parental stakes in public institutions -- specifically, witnessing Diego's teacher buying classroom supplies out of pocket, which prompted her voter registration at age 26-27 (Q23, Q23 follow-up). She participates in presidential elections but inconsistently in lower-ballot races, reflecting information-cost barriers; she researches candidates through trusted personal networks including Lupita, brother Luis, and church contacts rather than through party affiliation (Q23, Q23 follow-up). Her political orientation is issue-driven, clustering around affordable health care, adequately funded schools, cost of living, and fair treatment of immigrant-origin families, while holding culturally traditional positions on family and faith that create cross-pressures with either major party (Q24, Q95, Q96). She recently expanded her civic repertoire to include attending 4-5 Gwinnett County commission meetings, prompted by book club member Janet, and engages with local zoning debates affecting her neighborhood (Q25, Q25 follow-up). Her community organizing work -- moderating three Facebook groups, planning multicultural events, organizing potlucks that bring together Korean, Mexican, Indian, Black, and white families (Q08, Q48) -- functions as informal civic infrastructure even though she does not frame it politically. Her response to racial justice movements is empathetic and solidarity-oriented, acknowledging shared experiences of discrimination between Mexican and Black communities in Gwinnett County, but behaviorally cautious -- she promotes inclusion through event work and private conversations rather than protest (Q26, Q27, Q29). She navigates a persistent background awareness about immigration-related policing anxiety in the Mexican community, where some neighbors avoid calling police due to documentation fears (Q20, Q26 follow-up).
summaries.psychologist: Maria displays high conscientiousness through meticulous event budgeting, meal planning, and household management, paired with high agreeableness evident in her lifelong caretaking across family and community spheres -- a pattern originating in childhood when she became 'a second mom in the house' after dropping out of school to care for her brother while her mother worked two jobs (Q01, Q01 follow-up). Her self-concept centers on a caretaker identity that extends to her mother, children, church community, and neighborhood, accompanied by a persistent guilt pattern when she cannot meet these demands -- expressing feelings of 'failing' when unable to buy Diego shoes (Q64 follow-up) and guilt when others compensate during her illness (Q33 follow-up). A critical tension exists between this caretaker orientation and her emerging recognition of self-investment needs, catalyzed by the pivotal Dia de los Muertos festival that shifted her self-perception from 'just a helper' to a capable leader: 'I actually ran this whole thing... People came to me with questions and I had answers' (Q02 follow-up). Motherhood is her primary motivational axis: nearly every major decision is framed through her children's futures, from neighborhood stability for school continuity to pursuing the GED as modeling behavior for Diego and Sofia (Q01, Q108, Q109 follow-up). Her emotional regulation system handles routine stress through five functional coping mechanisms: cooking as emotional processing (making 60 tamales at midnight, Q38 follow-up), yoga breathing for acute chest-tightness episodes (Q45 follow-up), garden time as solitary morning restoration (Q43 follow-up), weekly honest check-in texts with Lupita (Q44 follow-up), and Mass attendance as spiritual centering (Q44 follow-up, Q47). However, when multiple role demands converge simultaneously -- as during the fall 2025 crisis combining Carlos's hour cuts, Diego's ethnic bullying at school, and her mother's health episode -- her system becomes overwhelmed, evidenced by the bathroom-floor crying episode where she was unable to speak when Carlos found her (Q44). Her attitude toward professional mental health help has shifted from cultural stigma ('you're supposed to be strong, pray about it,' Q45) to primarily practical barriers (cost, time, Spanish-speaking availability), reflecting generational change in her community; her friend Lupita's positive counseling experience has normalized the idea (Q45 follow-up).

Wrote down three life goals: GED by 35, job at community center, start catering business (Q03 follow-up)

sensory_and_physical_context.description: Non-clinical physical factors: chronic pain, mobility, sleep, dietary practices tied to non-preference reasons, family stress patterns
items: Chronic lower back pain for ~18 months, worse in mornings and after prolonged standing (cooking for events); manages with ibuprofen 2-3x/week and weekly yoga; has not seen a doctor due to cost (Q31, Q31 follow-up, Q34)
Persistent afternoon fatigue: suspects iron deficiency based on mother's similar experience; copes with coffee and sweets; has not obtained blood work (Q33, Q33 follow-up)

Poor sleep quality: lies awake ruminating about finances, children's futures, and mother's health; falls asleep quickly from exhaustion but wakes with racing thoughts; has tried yoga breathing with inconsistent success (Q43, Q43 follow-up)
Carries extra weight since pregnancies; acknowledges need for more activity but constrained by time and cost (Q31)
Tension headaches during high-stress periods; one episode lasted three days but refused ER due to cost (Q31, Q36)
Chest tightness episodes during acute financial or planning stress: 10-15 minute duration, managed with 4-count yoga breathing and stopping activity (Q45 follow-up)
High coffee consumption with sugar throughout the day as energy management (Q32 follow-up)
Does not smoke; drinks alcohol rarely (beer or wine at parties, maybe once every couple months); no substance use (Q38, Q39)
Carlos's stress pattern: becomes even quieter than usual, sleeps poorly, worries about providing for family (Q46)
Diego's stress response: becomes withdrawn, retreats to room, draws for hours (Q46)
Sofia's stress response: becomes clingy, wants to be right next to Maria constantly (Q46)

Family flu episode last winter knocked out entire household; Maria was in bed five days; Carlos missed two days of work; mother came over despite risk to help; Lupita delivered medicine (Q33, Q33 follow-up)

media_and_information_diet.description: News sources, social media platforms, entertainment formats, information-seeking behavior
items: Facebook (primary platform): manages 3 community groups, shares posts and resources, browses 1-2 hours daily for community management (Q48)
Instagram: posts food and garden photos, follows cooking and art accounts, ~30 min before bed (Q48 follow-up)
WhatsApp: multiple group chats for school, church, dance group, family -- primary communication tool for Mexican community (Q48)
YouTube: watches GED math tutorials from a specific creator she finds effective, plus cooking videos (Q18 follow-up)
Follows Atlanta Falcons and Hawks on social media (Q48 follow-up)
Gets health information from children's pediatrician (trusted) and government health websites (Q42)

Gets political information through personal network (Lupita, brother Luis, church ladies, book club member Janet) and phone research -- explicitly issue-driven rather than party-driven (Q23 follow-up)
Father Morales trusted for guidance on health decisions (vaccine advocacy) and community matters (Q42, Q42 follow-up)
Avoids conspiracy content, political arguments, and drama on social media; keeps feeds positive (Q48 follow-up)
Reads multicultural literary fiction through monthly book club: currently 'Americanah' by Chimamanda Ngozi Adichie; previously 'Beloved' by Toni Morrison (Q07 follow-up)

technology_and_tool_use.description: Device preferences, app usage, digital communication norms
items: Smartphone is primary and likely only personal device for social media, photography, communication, and learning
Uses phone calendar app to manage family schedules, children's activities, and event planning (started recently as life became busier, Q18)
Uses phone camera for food photography, event documentation, and everyday life captures; asked to photograph church events regularly (Q17 follow-up)
Plans events using physical notebook (budgets, vendor contacts, to-do lists) supplemented by phone for digital tasks
No computer or home office mentioned; works from kitchen table with notebook and phone (Q11 follow-up)
Carlos teases her about being 'addicted to my phone' (Q48 follow-up)
